title: W8: DDP, FSDP, and Efficient Attention
blurb: High priority for calculation-heavy computing questions. Summer 2025 asks DDP communication and FlashAttention directly.

knowledge_points.title: DDP Gradient Synchronization
knowledge_points.recap: DDP synchronizes gradients after backward, commonly via reduce-scatter plus all-gather. Be able to reason about chunks, ranks, and communication cost.

questions.q: For $r^2$ GPUs in a 2D mesh and N=$r^2$ m parameters, derive the reduce-scatter communication time using the two-phase ring algorithm.
questions.answer: Do a column-wise reduce-scatter across r ranks, then a row-wise reduce-scatter across r ranks. Phase 1 communicates $rm$ elements per rank for $r-1$ iterations: ($r-1$)($T_s$ + $rm$ $T_m$). Phase 2 communicates m elements per rank for $r-1$ iterations: ($r-1$)($T_s$ + m $T_m$). Total: ($r-1$)(2$T_s$ + (r+1)m $T_m$).
questions.extend: The exam also accepted a less efficient flat ring formula only if the path or partners were explicit.

questions.q: Why does DDP need gradient synchronization?
questions.answer: Each replica sees a different mini-batch, so its local gradient is only a partial estimate. Synchronizing, usually by summing or averaging gradients across replicas, makes all model copies apply the same update.

knowledge_points.title: FSDP/ZeRO and FlashAttention
knowledge_points.recap: FSDP and ZeRO reduce memory by sharding states. FlashAttention speeds attention by reducing memory traffic rather than changing the mathematical result.

questions.q: What are the two main techniques used by FlashAttention?
questions.answer: Tiling partitions Q, K, and V into blocks that fit in fast on-chip SRAM. Operator fusion combines score computation, softmax, and multiplication by V in one kernel, avoiding materializing large intermediate attention matrices in slow HBM.
questions.extend: The key idea is IO-awareness: same attention result, less memory traffic.

questions.q: What does ZeRO-2 shard and what does it keep replicated?
questions.answer: ZeRO-2 shards gradients and optimizer states across GPUs, but each GPU still stores the full forward/backward model weights. This is why the memory formula includes a replicated 2-byte weight te$rm$ plus sharded gradient/optimizer te$rm$s.
